questions.questionText: Hanging is differentiated from strangulation by:
questions.optionB: In hanging: ligature mark is oblique and above thyroid cartilage with non-continuous mark, internal injuries less severe; in strangulation: mark is horizontal and at/below thyroid with continuous mark
questions.explanation: Hanging: constricting force is body weight. Ligature mark: oblique (runs upward toward knot), above thyroid cartilage, NON-continuous (gap at knot point), pale/parchment-like. Usually suicidal. Fractures: hyoid bone (rare in young, common >40), cervical spine (judicial hanging — hangman's fracture C2). Strangulation/Ligature: external force. Mark: horizontal, at/below thyroid level, CONTINUOUS (encircles completely), deeper, congested. Usually homicidal. Manual strangulation: fingertip bruises, nail marks on neck. Reference: Reddy — Hanging vs Strangulation.

questions.questionText: Diatom test is used to diagnose:
questions.optionB: Drowning (ante-mortem — diatoms enter lungs, cross alveolar membrane, reach bone marrow, organs via bloodstream)
questions.explanation: Diatom test: confirmatory test for ante-mortem drowning. Diatoms: microscopic algae with siliceous (glass) cell wall, found in water. In live drowning: water aspirated → diatoms cross alveolar membrane → enter blood → deposited in distant organs (bone marrow, brain, liver, kidney). Post-mortem submersion: diatoms limited to lungs/stomach only (no circulation). Bone marrow: most reliable sample (sealed compartment, resistant to contamination). Acid digestion method to extract. Reference: Reddy — Drowning Diatom Test.

questions.questionText: Tardieu spots (petechial hemorrhages) in asphyxia are found on:
questions.optionB: Visceral pleura, pericardium, thymus, and conjunctivae — caused by capillary rupture due to venous congestion
questions.explanation: Tardieu spots (asphyxial petechiae): small, round, dark hemorrhagic spots on serosal surfaces — visceral pleura (most common), pericardium, thymus (in children), conjunctivae. Caused by: raised venous pressure → capillary rupture. Seen in all forms of asphyxia (especially compression, strangulation). Not specific to asphyxia alone (can occur in bleeding disorders, sepsis). Le Facie sympathique: sign of ante-mortem hanging (retracted upper eyelid). Amussat's sign: transverse tear of carotid intima. Reference: Reddy — Asphyxia Signs.
